title: Riving with a froe
subtitle: Splitting a green billet along the grain for blanks and staves
excerpt: Riving splits timber along its natural grain lines rather than sawing across them, producing blanks that are stronger along their length than sawn sections. A froe and beetle are the tools. This tutorial covers setting up for riving, reading the grain before the split, controlling the direction of a running split, and what to do when the split wanders.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. II (1883) for the froe-and-maul method in green timber. Newey & Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1898) for grain-reading in riven timber.

content.text: Riving is the process of splitting timber along its grain rather than sawing across it. A

blank follows the wood's natural grain lines precisely, which means the fibres run continuously from end to end without being cut. A sawn blank cuts across grain wherever the annual rings deviate from the saw line, producing weak short grain. For a spoon, a mallet handle, a chair stile, or any piece that will be stressed along its length, the riven blank is stronger. The technique is also faster than sawing for short sections of

Riving suits any green-wood project where the blank needs straight grain along its working axis: spoon billets, mallet heads, axe handles, chair stiles and stretchers, shaving-horse components, and willow stakes for basketry. It does not suit short-grain work, cross-grain shapes, or any project cut at an angle to the growth rings where riving would produce waste. For those, sawn blanks are more material-efficient.

Look at the end grain of the billet. The growth rings curve from pith to bark. The froe will split between the rings if driven from the outside toward the pith, or across the rings if driven from the edge. Across-ring splits (called tangential splits) produce staves that cup as they dry; radial splits (pith-to-bark) produce staves that stay flat. For most green-wood projects, aim for radial splits.

Check the side of the billet for spiral grain. Hold the billet upright and look along its length. If the grain lines run straight up and down, the split will run straight. If the grain lines spiral even slightly, the split will wander in the direction of the spiral. Billets with visible spiral grain are still workable but the split will need active steering.

Set the billet upright on a solid end-grain block or directly on the ground if the ground is firm and level. The block should be stable and knee-high, or the billet can be stood on its end on bare earth. The froe is placed on the end grain of the billet at the intended split line and driven in with the beetle until the blade is seated 3 to 4 cm deep.

Hold the froe blade on the end grain at the intended line. Strike the top of the blade with the beetle in a firm two-handed swing to drive the blade into the wood. One or two strikes are usually enough to set the blade. The blade should enter along the intended split line. If it starts to drift, the end grain grain lines will show the direction of natural weakness; follow them rather than fight them.

Once the froe blade is seated, use the vertical handle to lever and twist the split open. Pulling the handle down opens the near side of the split; pulling it toward you opens the far side. The split will run ahead of the froe blade in the direction of the grain. Watch the split front: if it runs toward one face, lever the handle in the opposite direction to draw it back toward the centre.

For longer billets, the split may need to be walked down the length in stages rather than opened in one movement. Walk the split by advancing the froe blade forward incrementally, using the handle to steer, and inserting splitting wedges if needed to hold the split open while you reposition. Green wood splits very easily; a 60 cm billet of sycamore or ash will split in less than a minute once the technique is established.
What a correct split looks like

A clean riven split follows the grain faithfully from end to end. The split face shows the natural wood texture along the grain, slightly rough and fibrous rather than smooth. Both halves are mirror images of each other. If one half has a visible groove running along its face and the other has a corresponding ridge, the split has followed the growth ring rather than crossing it, which is acceptable for most projects.

attrs.intro: What happens when the split goes wrong.

items.fix: As soon as the split front begins to wander, lever the froe handle firmly in the opposite direction. In severe cases, drive a splitting wedge into the stray face to force the split back toward the centre.
items.cause: Spiral grain or uneven density on one side pulling the split. The froe was not steered early enough.
items.symptom: The split runs off to one face and nearly exits through the side

items.fix: Drive the froe further forward past the obstruction and try to restart the split on the other side of it. If the obstruction is central, the billet may not be suitable for riving and should be sawn instead.
items.cause: A knot, brash patch, or interlocked grain is blocking the split front.
items.symptom: The split stops midway and the billet will not continue to open

items.fix: For the next riving operation, pencil a line across the end grain first, place the froe blade on the line exactly, and check alignment before striking.
items.cause: The froe blade was not placed on the true centre line of the end grain at the start.
items.symptom: The two halves are very different in thickness

attrs.heading: Common problems
